Moses stands at the threshold of death and addresses Israel one final time, and what he does is remarkable: he does not offer false hope or encourage them with inspiring words about their own strength. Instead, he makes an almost brutal pronouncement of truth. He will not cross the Jordan. The Lord has declared this, and it is final. Yet in that very finality lies the deepest comfort available—the promise that God Himself will go before them, that He will never leave them nor forsake them. This is not Moses' confidence in Israel or even in Joshua. It is God's promise, repeated to the people and directly to Joshua in the Lord's presence. The God who destroyed Sihon and Og, the kings of Amorites, will do the same for them. The conquest will not depend on military genius or human cunning. It will depend on God's faithfulness to His covenant with their fathers.

But Moses knows something the people need to know, and he will not shield them from it. God tells him plainly: after he is gone, they will turn to other gods. They will break the covenant. They will abandon Him. This is not speculation or fear—it is divine foreknowledge. The Lord is not surprised by what will happen; He already sees their rebellion before they even enter the land. So Moses ensures that a written record of the Law is placed beside the ark, not as decoration but as a witness against them. He commissions a song to be recited and remembered, a song that will testify to their faithlessness when the time comes. He calls heaven and earth as witnesses. These are the acts of a man who loves his people enough to tell them the truth about their own hearts.

What does it mean that the Lord knows Israel will rebel and yet brings them into the land anyway? It means God's plan is not contingent on human perfection or sustained obedience. The covenant was sworn to Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, not because those generations were perfectly faithful, but because God is faithful. The land will be theirs. They will prosper there. And when they turn to idolatry and face judgment, even that judgment will serve a purpose—it will become the context in which God's mercy and restoration become visible again. The Law written in a book, the song passed down through generations—these are God's way of ensuring that even Israel's failure becomes a testimony, that even their rebellion cannot erase the record of what He requires and what He promises.

Moses' final act before the song itself is to tell the leaders what he knows with absolute certainty: you will corrupt yourselves, and disaster will come. This is not discouragement meant to paralyze them; it is clarity meant to make them wise. A people who understands its own capacity for rebellion is a people that may yet return to God when affliction comes. The covenant will not be broken by their unfaithfulness—God's faithfulness stands regardless. But they will experience the consequences of their choices, and in those consequences, they will find either their destruction or their repentance. The Lord does not hide from them what is to come. He faces it directly, and so must they.
